}} | country_largest_city = {{flagcountry|Tokyo}} | country_largest_city_population = 14,043,239 | population = 104,000,000 |timezone1 = {{ill|Japan Standard Time|en|Japan Standard Time|qid=Q909085|lt=Japan Standard Time|1=Japan Standard Time}} ([[:en:UTC+9|UTC+9]]) | population_as_of = 2017 | density_km2 = 447 | ethnic_groups = {{ill|Japanese people|en|Japanese people|qid=Q161652|lt=Japanese|1=Japanese people}} }} {{nihongo|'''Honshu'''||Honshū|{{IPA|ja|hoꜜɰ̃ɕɯː|pron|ja-Honshu.ogg}}; {{lit|main [[Islands of Japan|island]]|lk=yes}}}}, historically called {{Nihongo|'''Akitsushima'''|秋津島||{{lit|Dragonfly island}}}},{{Cite web |url=https://etc.usf.edu/maps/galleries/asia/japan/index.php |title=Maps ETC - Asia -> Japan |publisher=[[University of South Florida]] |access-date=25 June 2023}}{{Cite web |url=http://kojiki.kokugakuin.ac.jp/shinmei/oyamatotoyoakizushima/ |title=Ōyamatotoyoakizushima |publisher=Kokugakuin University |access-date=29 June 2023}}{{Cite web |url=https://isahaya-jinja.jp/archives/11079 |title=Akitsushima |publisher=Isahaya Shrine |access-date=29 June 2023}} is the [[Japanese archipelago|largest and most populous island]] of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}.{{cite web |title=離島とは(島の基礎知識) (what is a remote island?) |publisher=[[Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism]] |url=http://www.mlit.go.jp/crd/chirit/ritoutoha.html |website=MLIT (Ministry of Land, Infrastructure, Transport and Tourism) |date=22 August 2015 |access-date=9 August 2019 |language=ja |format=website |quote=MILT classification 6,852 islands(main islands: 5 islands, remote islands: 6,847 islands) |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20071113053915/http://www.mlit.go.jp/crd/chirit/ritoutoha.html |archive-date=2007-11-13}}{{Cite web|title=Honshu|website=[[Encyclopædia Britannica]]|url=https://www.britannica.com/place/Honshu|access-date=19 February 2016}} It is located south of [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] across the {{ill|Tsugaru Strait|en|Tsugaru Strait|qid=Q271521|lt=Tsugaru Strait|1=Tsugaru Strait}}, north of [[Shikoku]] across the [[Seto Inland Sea|Inland Sea]], and northeast of [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] across the {{ill|Kanmon Straits|en|Kanmon Straits|qid=Q494894|lt=Kanmon Straits|1=Kanmon Straits}}. The island separates the {{ill|Sea of Japan|en|Sea of Japan|qid=Q27092|lt=Sea of Japan|1=Sea of Japan}}, which lies to its north and west, from the [[:en:North Pacific Ocean|North Pacific Ocean]] to the south and east. It is the {{ill|en:List of islands by area|en|en:List of islands by area|qid=Q104176|lt=seventh-largest island|1=List of islands by area}} in the world, and the {{ill|en:List of islands by population|en|en:List of islands by population|qid=Q911018|lt=second-most populous|1=List of islands by population}} after the {{ill|List of islands of Indonesia|en|List of islands of Indonesia|qid=Q1142361|lt=Indonesian|1=List of islands of Indonesia}} island of {{ill|Java|en|Java|qid=Q3757|lt=Java|1=Java}}.[http://www.soumu.go.jp/main_sosiki/jichi_gyousei/c-gyousei/daityo/index.html Japan Civil Registry Database 2013]See [http://www.stat.go.jp/english/data/kokusei/2000/final/hyodai.htm Japan Census of 2000]; the editors of {{ill|en:List of islands by population|en|en:List of islands by population|qid=Q911018|lt=en:List of islands by population|1=List of islands by population}} appear to have used similar data from the relevant statistics bureaux and totalled up the various administrative districts that make up each island, and then done the same for less populous islands. An editor of this article has not repeated that work. Therefore this plausible and eminently reasonable Ranking is posted as unsourced [[Wikipedia:Common knowledge|common knowledge]].{{cite web |url=http://islands.unep.ch/Tiarea.htm |title=Islands By Land Area |publisher=Islands.unep.ch |access-date=2010-08-01 |archive-date=2018-02-20 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20180220003634/http://islands.unep.ch/Tiarea.htm |url-status=dead }} Honshu had a population of 104 million {{As of|2017|lc=y}}, constituting 81.3% of the entire population of Japan,{{cite book|last=Boquet|first=Yves|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=90C4DgAAQBAJ&pg=PA16|title=The Philippine Archipelago|publisher=Springer|year=2017|isbn=9783319519265|page=16}} and mostly concentrated in the coastal areas and plains. Approximately 30% of the total population resides in the {{ill|Greater Tokyo Area|en|Greater Tokyo Area|qid=Q328121|lt=Greater Tokyo Area|1=Greater Tokyo Area}} on the {{ill|Kantō Plain|en|Kantō Plain|qid=Q1124865|lt=Kantō Plain|1=Kantō Plain}}. As the historical center of Japanese cultural and political power,{{Cite book|last1=Dolan|first1=Ronald|title=Japan: a country study|last2=Worden|first2=Robert|publisher=Federal Research Division, Library of Congress|year=1992}} the island includes several past Japanese capitals, including {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyōto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Nara (city)|en|Nara (city)|qid=Q169134|lt=Nara|1=Nara (city)}}, and {{ill|Kamakura|en|Kamakura|qid=Q200267|lt=Kamakura|1=Kamakura}}. Much of the island's southern shore forms part of the {{ill|Taiheiyō Belt|en|Taiheiyō Belt|qid=Q44006|lt=Taiheiyō Belt|1=Taiheiyō Belt}}, a {{ill|megalopolis|en|megalopolis|qid=Q471822|lt=megalopolis|1=Megalopolis}} that spans several of the Japanese islands. Honshu contains Japan's highest mountain, Mount Fuji, and its largest lake, {{ill|Lake Biwa|en|Lake Biwa|qid=Q200239|lt=Lake Biwa|1=Lake Biwa}}.{{Cite web|title=Honshu {{!}} Facts, History, & Points of Interest|url=https://www.britannica.com/place/Honshu|access-date=2021-04-14|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}} Most of Japan's industry is located in a belt running along Honshu's southern coast, from {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} to {{ill|Nagoya|en|Nagoya|qid=Q11751|lt=Nagoya|1=Nagoya}}, {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|qid=Q34600|lt=Kyōto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}}, {{ill|Kobe|en|Kobe|qid=Q48320|lt=Kobe|1=Kobe}}, and {{ill|Hiroshima|en|Hiroshima|qid=Q34664|lt=Hiroshima|1=Hiroshima}}; by contrast, the economy along the northwestern Sea of Japan coast is largely based on fishing and agriculture.''{{ill|Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan|en|Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan|qid=Q488777|lt=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan|1=Kodansha Encyclopedia of Japan}}'' The island is linked to the other three major Japanese islands by a number of bridges and tunnels. The island primarily shares two climates, with Northern Honshu having a mainly {{ill|Humid continental climate|en|Humid continental climate|qid=Q10856115|lt=Humid continental climate|1=Humid continental climate}} while the south has a {{ill|Humid subtropical climate|en|Humid subtropical climate|qid=Q864320|lt=Humid subtropical climate|1=Humid subtropical climate}}.{{cite journal |last=Köppen |first=Wladimir |date=1884 |title=Die Wärmezonen der Erde, nach der Dauer der heissen, gemässigten und kalten Zeit und nach der Wirkung der Wärme auf die organische Welt betrachtet |url=https://boris.unibe.ch/9257/1/s9.pdf |trans-title=The thermal zones of the earth according to the duration of hot, moderate and cold periods and to the impact of heat on the organic world) |journal=Meteorologische Zeitschrift |translator1-first=E. |translator1-last=Volken |translator2-first=S |translator2-last=Brönnimann |publication-date=2011 |volume=20 |issue=3 |pages=351–360 |via=ingentaconnect.com/content/schweiz/mz/2011/00000020/00000003/art00009 |bibcode=2011MetZe..20..351K |doi=10.1127/0941-2948/2011/105 |s2cid=209855204 |access-date=2016-09-02 }} ==Etymology== The name of the island, {{nihongo|Honshū|本州}}, directly translates to "main province" or "original land" in English. ==History== ===Early history=== Humans first arrived in Honshu approximately 37,000 years ago, and likely earlier. The first humans to arrive in Honshu were {{ill|Stone Age|en|Stone Age|qid=Q11759|lt=Stone Age|1=Stone Age}} hunter-gatherers from Northeast Asia, likely following the migration of {{ill|ice age|en|ice age|qid=Q49367|lt=ice age|1=Ice age}} {{ill|Megafauna|en|Megafauna|qid=Q730371|lt=Megafauna|1=Megafauna}}. Surviving artifacts from this period include finely-crafted stone blades, similar to those found in {{ill|Siberia|en|Siberia|qid=Q5428|lt=Siberia|1=Siberia}}.{{cite web | url=https://aboutjapan.japansociety.org/content.cfm/early_japan_50000bc_710ad | title=About Japan: A Teacher's Resource | Early Japan (50,000 BC - 710 AD) | Japan Society }} ===Meiji Restoration=== {{Empty section|date=February 2023}} ===World War II=== Honshu was the target of devastating {{ill|Air raids on Japan|en|Air raids on Japan|qid=Q590545|lt=air raids|1=Air raids on Japan}} during the {{ill|Pacific Ocean theater of World War II|en|Pacific Ocean theater of World War II|qid=Q2920892|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific Ocean theater of World War II}} of {{ill|Pacific War|en|Pacific War|qid=Q184425|lt=Pacific War|1=Pacific War}}. The first air raid to strike Honshu and the other home islands was the {{ill|Doolittle Raid|en|Doolittle Raid|qid=Q713516|lt=Doolittle Raid|1=Doolittle Raid}}. With the introduction of the {{ill|Boeing B-29 Superfortress|en|Boeing B-29 Superfortress|qid=Q184870|lt=Boeing B-29 Superfortress|1=Boeing B-29 Superfortress}}, the firebombing of Tokyo culminated in [[:en:Operation Meetinghouse|Operation Meetinghouse]], the most destructive air raid in human history, which destroyed {{convert|16|sqmi|km2 acre}} of central {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}}, leaving an estimated 100,000 civilians dead, and over one million homeless.{{cite magazine |url=https://www.wired.com/2011/03/0309incendiary-bombs-kill-100000-tokyo/ |title=March 9, 1945: Burning the Heart Out of the Enemy |last=Long |first=Tony |date=9 March 2011 |magazine=[[Wired (magazine)|Wired]] |quote=1945: In the single deadliest air raid of World War II, 330 American B-29s rain incendiary bombs on Tokyo, touching off a firestorm that kills upwards of 100,000 people, burns a quarter of the city to the ground, and leaves a million homeless.}} The war ended with the [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki#Hiroshima|atomic bombings of Hiroshima]] and [[Atomic bombings of Hiroshima and Nagasaki#Nagasaki|Nagasaki]] shortly before Japan's surrender and signing of the {{ill|Japanese Instrument of Surrender|en|Japanese Instrument of Surrender|qid=Q665554|lt=Japanese Instrument of Surrender|1=Japanese Instrument of Surrender}} on September 2, 1945, on board the {{USS|Missouri|BB-63}} in {{ill|Tokyo Bay|en|Tokyo Bay|qid=Q141017|lt=Tokyo Bay|1=Tokyo Bay}}. ==Geography== [[File:Satellite_View_of_Japan_1999.jpg|thumb|200x255px|left|Japan as seen from a satellite. Honshu is the largest, middle island.]] The island is roughly {{convert|1300|km|abbr=on}} long and ranges from {{convert|50|to|230|km|mi|abbr=on}} wide, and its total area is {{convert|227,960|km²|sqmi|abbr=on}}, making it slightly larger than the island of {{ill|Great Britain|en|Great Britain|qid=Q23666|lt=Great Britain|1=Great Britain}}. Its land area has been increasing with {{ill|Land reclamation|en|Land reclamation|qid=Q1130322|lt=Land reclamation|1=Land reclamation}} and coastal uplift in the north due to {{ill|plate tectonics|en|plate tectonics|qid=Q7950|lt=plate tectonics|1=Plate tectonics}} with a {{ill|convergent boundary|en|convergent boundary|qid=Q1255282|lt=convergent boundary|1=Convergent boundary}}. Honshu has {{convert|10084|km|0}} of coastline. Mountainous and volcanic, Honshu experiences frequent earthquakes (such as the [[:en:1923 Great Kantō earthquake|Great Kantō earthquake]], which heavily damaged Tokyo in September 1923; and the {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|qid=Q36204|lt=earthquake of March 2011|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}}, which moved the northeastern part of the island by varying amounts of as much as {{convert|5.3|m|ft|abbr=on}}{{cite web|title=Map of Horizontal Land Movement caused by 2011/3/11 M9.0 earthquake|url=http://www.gsi.go.jp/common/000059672.pdf|date=March 19, 2011|publisher=[[Geospatial Information Authority of Japan]]|access-date=15 November 2012|language=ja}}{{cite news|title=Quake shifted Japan by over two meters|url=http://www.dw-world.de/dw/article/0,,14909967,00.html|publisher=[[Deutsche Welle]]|date=March 14, 2011|access-date=March 14, 2011}} while causing devastating tsunamis). The highest peak is the active volcano {{ill|Mount Fuji|en|Mount Fuji|qid=Q39231|lt=Mount Fuji|1=Mount Fuji}} at {{convert|3776|m|ft|0|abbr=on}}, which makes Honshu the world's {{ill|List of islands by highest point|en|List of islands by highest point|qid=Q1381625|lt=7th highest island|1=List of islands by highest point}}. There are many rivers, including the {{ill|Shinano River|en|Shinano River|qid=Q734455|lt=Shinano River|1=Shinano River}}, Japan's longest. The {{ill|Japanese Alps|en|Japanese Alps|qid=Q645773|lt=Japanese Alps|1=Japanese Alps}} span the width of Honshu, from the 'Sea of Japan' coast to the Pacific shore. The climate is generally [[:en:humid subtropical|humid subtropical]] in western Japan and [[:en:humid continental|humid continental]] in the north. ===Population=== Honshu has a total population of 104 million people, according to a 2017 estimate, 81.3% of the entire population of Japan. The largest city is {{ill|Tokyo|en|Tokyo|qid=Q1490|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo}} (population: 13,988,129),{{Cite web|title=Tokyo Population 2021 (Demographics, Maps, Graphs)|url=https://worldpopulationreview.com/world-cities/tokyo-population|access-date=2021-10-22|website=worldpopulationreview.com}} the capital of Japan and part of the {{ill|Greater Tokyo Area|en|Greater Tokyo Area|qid=Q328121|lt=Greater Tokyo Area|1=Greater Tokyo Area}}, the most populous {{ill|Metropolitan area|en|Metropolitan area|qid=Q1907114|lt=Metropolitan area|1=Metropolitan area}} in the world. ===Extreme points=== ====Bridges and tunnels==== Honshu is connected to the islands of Hokkaidō, [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]] and Shikoku by tunnels and bridges. Three bridge systems have been built across the islands of the Inland Sea between Honshu and Shikoku ({{ill|Akashi Kaikyo Bridge|en|Akashi Kaikyo Bridge|qid=Q184213|lt=Akashi Kaikyo Bridge|1=Akashi Kaikyo Bridge}} and the {{ill|Ōnaruto Bridge|en|Ōnaruto Bridge|qid=Q1292175|lt=Ōnaruto Bridge|1=Ōnaruto Bridge}}; [[Shin-Onomichi Bridge]], {{ill|Innoshima Bridge|en|Innoshima Bridge|qid=Q3019822|lt=Innoshima Bridge|1=Innoshima Bridge}}, [[Ikuchi Bridge]], {{ill|Tatara Bridge|en|Tatara Bridge|qid=Q1539750|lt=Tatara Bridge|1=Tatara Bridge}}, [[Ōmishima Bridge]], {{ill|Hakata–Ōshima Bridge|en|Hakata–Ōshima Bridge|qid=Q1799188|lt=Hakata–Ōshima Bridge|1=Hakata–Ōshima Bridge}}, and the {{ill|Kurushima Kaikyō Bridge|en|Kurushima Kaikyō Bridge|qid=Q2740666|lt=Kurushima Kaikyō Bridge|1=Kurushima Kaikyō Bridge}}; [[:en:Shimotsui-Seto Bridge|Shimotsui-Seto Bridge]], [[:en:Hitsuishijima Bridge|Hitsuishijima Bridge]], [[:en:Iwakurojima Bridge|Iwakurojima Bridge]], [[:en:Yoshima Bridge|Yoshima Bridge]], [[:en:Kita Bisan-Seto Bridge|Kita Bisan-Seto Bridge]], and the [[:en:Minami Bisan-Seto Bridge|Minami Bisan-Seto Bridge]]), the {{ill|Seikan Tunnel|en|Seikan Tunnel|qid=Q99250|lt=Seikan Tunnel|1=Seikan Tunnel}} connects Honshu with Hokkaidō, and the [[:en:Kanmonkyo Bridge|Kanmonkyo Bridge]] and {{ill|Kanmon Railway Tunnel|en|Kanmon Railway Tunnel|qid=Q15972411|lt=Kanmon Tunnel|1=Kanmon Railway Tunnel}} connect Honshu with [[Kyushu|Kyūshū]]. ===Flora and fauna=== These are notable flora and fauna of Honshu. :{| class="wikitable sortable" |+ Notable flora and faunaJapanese Wiki page [[:ja:北海道]] |- ! Name !! Type ! Notes |- | {{ill|Japanese black bear|en|Japanese black bear|qid=Q11954115|lt=Japanese black bear|1=Japanese black bear}} | Fauna | A subspecies of the {{ill|Asian black bear|en|Asian black bear|qid=Q124410|lt=Asian black bear|1=Asian black bear}}. It is typically [[:en:herbivorous|herbivorous]] and lives in Honshu and Kyushu. |- |{{ill|Japanese macaque|en|Japanese macaque|qid=Q191781|lt=Japanese macaque|1=Japanese macaque}} |Fauna |(''Macaca fuscata'' or snow monkey), is a terrestrial {{ill|Old World monkey|en|Old World monkey|qid=Q182968|lt=Old World monkey|1=Old World monkey}} species that is native to {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|qid=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. |- | [[:en:Japanese golden eagle|Japanese golden eagle]] | Fauna | (''Aquila chrysaetos japonica''), a subspecies of the {{ill|golden eagle|en|golden {{ill|Yakushima|en|Yakushima|qid=Q242176|lt=Yakushima|1=Yakushima}}. |} ===Geologic activity=== {{See also|Category:Volcanoes of Honshu}} Being on the {{ill|Ring of Fire|en|Ring of Fire|qid=Q18783|lt=Ring of Fire|1=Ring of Fire}}, the island of Honshu is seismically active, and is home to 40 active volcanoes. In 2011, an {{ill|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|en|2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami|qid=Q36204|lt=earthquake of magnitude 9.0–9.1|1=2011 Tōhoku earthquake and tsunami}} occurred off the coast of Honshu, generating tsunami waves up to 40.5 meters (133 ft) high and killing 19,747. It was the most powerful {{ill|List of earthquakes in Japan|en|List of earthquakes in Japan|qid=Q543692|lt=earthquake ever recorded in Japan|1=List of earthquakes in Japan}}, and the [[:en:Largest earthquakes by magnitude|fourth most powerful earthquake in the world]] since modern record-keeping began in 1900.{{cite news|title=New USGS number puts Japan quake at fourth largest |url=https://www.cbsnews.com/stories/2011/03/14/501364/main20043126.shtml |publisher=[[CBS News]] |agency=Associated Press |date=14 March 2011 |access-date=15 March 2011 Park|qid=Q3133264|lt=Biwako|1=Biwako Quasi-National Park}}, {{ill|Murō-Akame-Aoyama Quasi-National Park|en|Murō-Akame-Aoyama Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1072834|lt=Murō-Akame-Aoyama|1=Murō-Akame-Aoyama Quasi-National Park}}, {{ill|Kongō-Ikoma-Kisen Quasi-National Park|en|Kongō-Ikoma-Kisen Quasi-National Park|qid=Q727034|lt=Kongō-Ikoma-Kisen|1=Kongō-Ikoma-Kisen Quasi-National Park}}, {{ill|Yamato-Aogaki Quasi-National Park|en|Yamato-Aogaki Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1071168|lt=Yamato-Aogaki|1=Yamato-Aogaki Quasi-National Park}}, {{ill|Kōya-Ryūjin Quasi-National Park|en|Kōya-Ryūjin Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1071006|lt=Kōya-Ryūjin|1=Kōya-Ryūjin Quasi-National Park}}, {{ill|Meiji no Mori Minō Quasi-National Park|en|Meiji no Mori Minō Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1070991|lt=Meiji no Mori Minō|1=Meiji no Mori Minō Quasi-National Park}}, [[:en:Kyoto Tanba Kogen Quasi-National Park|Kyoto Tanba Kogen]] |- | {{ill|Chūgoku region|en|Chūgoku region|qid=Q127864|lt=Chūgoku|1=Chūgoku region}} || {{ill|Hyōnosen-Ushiroyama-Nagisan Quasi-National Park|en|Hyōnosen-Ushiroyama-Nagisan Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1072842|lt=Hyōnosen-Ushiroyama-Nagisan|1=Hyōnosen-Ushiroyama-Nagisan Quasi-National Park}}, {{ill|Hiba-Dogo-Taishaku Quasi-National Park|en|Hiba-Dogo-Taishaku Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1072908|lt=Hiba-Dōgo-Taishaku|1=Hiba-Dogo-Taishaku Quasi-National Park}}, {{ill|Nishi-Chugoku Sanchi Quasi-National Park|en|Nishi-Chugoku Sanchi Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1072818|lt=Nishi-Chūgoku Sanchi|1=Nishi-Chugoku Sanchi Quasi-National Park}}, {{ill|Kita-Nagato Kaigan Quasi-National Park|en|Kita-Nagato Kaigan Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1072783|lt=Kita-Nagato Kaigan|1=Kita-Nagato Kaigan Quasi-National Park}}, {{ill|Akiyoshidai Quasi-National Park|en|Akiyoshidai Quasi-National Park|qid=Q1072847|lt=Akiyoshidai|1=Akiyoshidai Quasi-National Park}} |- |} ==Economy== Honshu island generates around [[:en:US$|US$]]3.5 trillion or more than 80% of Japan's [[:en:GDP|GDP]].[http://stats.oecd.org/ Regions and Cities > Regional Statistics > Regional Economy > Gross Domestic Product, Large regions TL2], OECD.Stats. Accessed on 30 August 2022. === Agriculture === Fruit, vegetables, grains, rice and cotton make up the main produce grown in Honshu. The {{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|qid=Q129465|lt=Tohoku|1=Tōhoku region}} region, spanning the north-eastern part of the island, is notable for its rice production, with 65% of cultivated land being rice paddy fields – almost a quarter of all paddy fields in Japan.{{Cite web|title=Regions of Japan|url=https://web-japan.org/factsheet/en/pdf/02RegionsofJap.pdf|access-date=2021-10-22|website=Web Japan}} {{ill|Chiba Prefecture|en|Chiba Prefecture|qid=Q80011|lt=Chiba Prefecture|1=Chiba Prefecture}} is famous for its peanuts, also being the largest producer in Japan.{{Cite web|title=Peanuts {{!}} Authentic Japanese product|url=https://japan-brand.jnto.go.jp/foods/other_food/44/#:~:text=It%20may%20be%20surprising%20for,producer%20of%20peanuts%20in%20Japan.&text=Chiba%20peanuts%20have%20a%20rich,that%20use%20the%20local%20peanuts.|access-date=2021-10-22|website=japan-brand.jnto.go.jp}} Rare species of the lichen genus {{ill|Menegazzia caviisidia|en|Menegazzia caviisidia|qid=Q6816804|lt=Menegazzia|1=Menegazzia caviisidia}} are found only in Honshu.{{cite journal|vauthors=Bjerke JW|year=2004|title=Revision of the lichen genus Menegazzia in Japan, including two new species|journal=The Lichenologist|volume=36|issue=1|pages=15–25|doi=10.1017/S0024282904013878|issn=0024-2829|s2cid=85436634}} === Industry === Most of Japan's tea and silk is from Honshu.{{Cite web|date=2012|title=Honshu|url=http://www.infoplease.com/encyclopedia/world/honshu.html|access-date=2014-11-23|website=infoplease.com}} Japan's three largest industrial regions are all located on Honshu: the {{ill|Keihin region|en|Keihin region|qid=Q1197661|lt=Keihin region|1=Keihin region}}, the {{ill|Hanshin Industrial Region|en|Hanshin Industrial Region|qid=Q1038703|lt=Hanshin Industrial Region|1=Hanshin Industrial Region}}, and the {{ill|Chūkyō Industrial Area|en|Chūkyō Industrial Area|qid=Q5119025|lt=Chūkyō Industrial Area|1=Chūkyō Industrial Area}}. === Minerals and fuels === Honshu is home to a large portion{{Cite book|last=|first=|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WGsEAAAAMAAJ&dq=Natural+resources+in+honshu&pg=PA44|title=Natural Resources of Japan|publisher=General Headquarters, Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers, Natural Resources Section|year=1947|pages=42–48|language=English}} of Japan's minimal mineral reserves,{{Cite web|title=Japan – Resources and power|url=https://www.britannica.com/place/Japan|access-date=2021-10-22|website=Encyclopedia Britannica|language=en}} including small oil and coal deposits. Several coal deposits are located in the northern part of the island,{{Cite web|title=Catalogue of Geological Maps｜Geological Survey of Japan/ AIST|url=https://www.gsj.jp/Map/EN/geo-resources2.html|access-date=2021-10-22|website=www.gsj.jp}} concentrated in {{ill|Fukushima Prefecture|en|Fukushima Prefecture|qid=Q71707|lt=Fukushima Prefecture|1=Fukushima Prefecture}} and {{ill|Niigata Prefecture|en|Niigata Prefecture|qid=Q132705|lt=Niigata Prefecture|1=Niigata Prefecture}}, though Honshu's coal production is negligible in comparison to [[:en:Hokkaidō|Hokkaidō]] and [[Kyushu]].{{Cite book|last=|first=|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WGsEAAAAMAAJ|title=Natural Resources of Japan|date=1947|publisher=General Headquarters, Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers, Natural Resources Section|pages=44|language=English}} Most of Japan's oil reserves are also located in northern Honshu, along the west coast, spanning Niigata, {{ill|Yamagata Prefecture|en|Yamagata Prefecture|qid=Q125863|lt=Yamagata|1=Yamagata Prefecture}}, and {{ill|Akita Prefecture|en|Akita Prefecture|qid=Q81863|lt=Akita|1=Akita Prefecture}} Prefectures.{{Cite book|last=|first=|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WGsEAAAAMAAJ|title=Natural Resources of Japan|publisher=General Headquarters, Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers, Natural Resources Section|year=1947|pages=43|language=English}} Most of Japan's copper, lead, zinc and chromite is located on Honshu, along with smaller, scattered deposits of gold, silver, arsenic, sulfur and {{ill|pyrite|en|pyrite|qid=Q50769|lt=pyrite|1=Pyrite}}.{{Cite book|last=|first=|url=https://books.google.com/books?id=WGsEAAAAMAAJ|title=Natural Resources of Japan|date=1947|publisher=General Headquarters, Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers, Natural Resources Section|pages=44–45|language=English}} ==Transportation== The {{ill|Tokaido Shinkansen|en|Tokaido Shinkansen|qid=Q660895|lt=Tokaido Shinkansen|1=Tokaido Shinkansen}}, opened in 1964 between {{ill|Tokyo Station|en|Tokyo Station|qid=Q283196|lt=Tokyo|1=Tokyo Station}} and {{ill|Shin-Ōsaka Station|en|Shin-Ōsaka Station|qid=Q801438|lt=Shin-Ōsaka|1=Shin-Ōsaka Station}}, is Japan's first high-speed rail line.{{cite web|url=https://www.trainspread.com/japan/shinkansen/#:~:text=Opened%20in%201964%2C%20Tokaido%20Shinkansen,Railway%20Company%20(JR%20Central) |title=Shinkansen – Bullet Trains in Japan|website=Trainspread.com |date=2020 |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20200321092401/https://www.trainspread.com/japan/shinkansen/ |archive-date=March 21, 2020}} It is the world's oldest high-speed rail line and one of the most heavily used.{{Cite web |last=Kasai |first=Yoshiyuki |date=4 September 2010 |title=Bullet Train & Maglev System to Cross the Pacific |url=https://www.envoy.jp/2010/09/bullet-train-maglev-system-to-cross-the-pacific/ |url-status=dead |archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20120331013737/https://www.envoy.jp/2010/09/bullet-train-maglev-system-to-cross-the-pacific/ |archive-date=31 March 2012 |access-date=16 July 2022 |website=Envoy Media}}{{Cite web |title=Central Japan Railway Company |url=https://global.jr-central.co.jp/en/index.html |access-date=2022-07-16 |website=Central Japan Railway Company |language=ja}} The {{ill|San'yō Shinkansen|en|San'yō Shinkansen|qid=Q615964|lt=San'yō Shinkansen|1=San'yō Shinkansen}}, connects the two largest cities in western Japan, {{ill|Shin-Ōsaka Station|en|Shin-Ōsaka Station|qid=Q801438|lt=Shin-Osaka|1=Shin-Ōsaka Station}} in {{ill|Osaka|en|Osaka|qid=Q35765|lt=Osaka|1=Osaka}} with {{ill|Hakata Station|en|Hakata Station|qid=Q800875|lt=Hakata Station|1=Hakata Station}} in {{ill|Fukuoka|en|Fukuoka|qid=Q26600|lt=Fukuoka|1=Fukuoka}}. Both the Tokaido Shinkansen and the Sanyo Shinkansen help form a continuous high-speed railway through the {{ill|Taiheiyō Belt|en|Taiheiyō Belt|qid=Q44006|lt=Taiheiyō Belt|1=Taiheiyō Belt}} megalopolis. [[File:Series-N700a-Mt.Fuji.jpg|right|thumb|Mt. Fuji and the Tokaido Shinkansen]] ==Administrative regions and prefectures== The island is divided into five nominal regions and contains 34 prefectures, including metropolitan Tokyo. Administratively, some smaller islands are included within these prefectures, notably including the {{ill|Ogasawara Islands|en|Ogasawara Islands|qid=Q13383295|lt=Ogasawara Islands|1=Ogasawara Islands}}, [[Sado Island]], {{ill|Izu Ōshima|en|Izu Ōshima|qid=Q1070580|lt=Izu Ōshima|1=Izu Ōshima}}, and {{ill|Awaji Island|en|Awaji Island|qid=Q504373|lt=Awaji Island|1=Awaji Island}}. The regions and their prefectures are: {{colbegin}} *'''{{ill|Tōhoku region|en|Tōhoku region|qid=Q129465|lt=Tōhoku region|1=Tōhoku region}}''' consists of six prefectures. **{{flagcountry|Akita}} **{{flagcountry|Aomori}} **{{flagcountry|Fukushima}} **{{flagcountry|Iwate}} **{{flagcountry|Miyagi}} **{{flagcountry|Yamagata}} *'''{{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|qid=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}}''' consists of seven prefectures, including the capital of Japan which is the Tokyo Metropolis. **{{flagcountry|Chiba}} **{{flagcountry|Gunma}} **{{flagcountry|Ibaraki}} **{{flagcountry|Kanagawa}} **{{flagcountry|Saitama}} **{{flagcountry|Tochigi}} **{{flagcountry|Tokyo}} *'''{{ill|Chūbu region|en|Chūbu region|qid=Q134638|lt=Chūbu region|1=Chūbu region}}''' consists of nine prefectures. **{{flagcountry|Aichi}} **{{flagcountry|Fukui}} **{{flagcountry|Gifu}} **{{flagcountry|Ishikawa}} **{{flagcountry|Nagano}} **{{flagcountry|Niigata}} **{{flagcountry|Shizuoka}} **{{flagcountry|Toyama}} **{{flagcountry|Yamanashi}} *'''{{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|qid=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}}''' consists of seven prefectures. **{{flagcountry|Hyōgo}} **{{flagcountry|Kyoto}} **{{flagcountry|Mie}} **{{flagcountry|Nara}} **{{flagcountry|Osaka}} **{{flagcountry|Shiga}} **{{flagcountry|Wakayama}} *'''{{ill|Chūgoku region|en|Chūgoku region|qid=Q127864|lt=Chūgoku region|1=Chūgoku region}}''' consists of five prefectures. **{{flagcountry|Hiroshima}} **{{flagcountry|Okayama}} **{{flagcountry|Shimane}} **{{flagcountry|Tottori}}
